open_questions: Through-cycle homebuilding normalization and lot-option sizing remain widest judgment bands.
Buyback discipline above intrinsic value requires human capital-allocation review.
No approved third-party sources in base return.
thesis: NVR, Inc. is a U.S. homebuilder operating Ryan Homes, NVHomes, and Heartland Homes in the Mid-Atlantic and Southeast. **[Fact]** Unlike land-heavy peers, NVR controls finished lots through lot-purchase agreements (LPAs) with forfeitable cash deposits rather than owning most land on balance sheet. **[Fact]** FY2025 net income was **$1.340 billion**; homebuilding pretax income was **$1.610 billion** and mortgage-banking pretax income was **$152 million**; aggregate cash was about **$1.916 billion** against **$909 million** of senior notes (`NVR/research/evidence_reconciliation_2026-07-15.json`, NVR 2025 Form 10-K). **[Fact]** At year-end 2025 NVR controlled roughly **207,000** lots and carried **$851 million** of net contract-land deposits after a **$111 million** allowance.
why_market_wrong: NVR's capital-light lot option can look like peak-cycle earnings at a fair price when deposit impairments are rising. **[Inference]** FY2025 deposit impairment charges of **$75.9 million** (versus **$7.2 million** in 2024) and a **17%** cancellation rate show affordability stress, yet the stock near **$6,498** trades close to Marvin's **~$6,550** component base case. **[Inference]** The market may correctly price limited upside from here while under-appreciating how quickly NVR can abandon lots and preserve balance-sheet flexibility relative to land-heavy builders, or it may be right that normalized owner earnings are below peak 2025 GAAP.
